segments.title: The T.3 Velos and its Greek Navy mission
segments.summary: The T.3 Velos was a two-seat Blackburn torpedo aircraft built for the Greek Navy's coastal-defence, reconnaissance, bombing, and training duties.
segments.body: The Velos developed the Blackburn Dart into the third aircraft in the firm's torpedo-aircraft series. Designed in 1925 for the Greek Navy, it had to combine torpedo delivery with reconnaissance, bombing, and training. It was primarily a seaplane, but could receive a wheeled undercarriage when required, unlike the Dart's deck-landing-oriented arrangement.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Velos as a two-seat Dart development for a Greek Navy coastal-defence role and contrasts its seaplane configuration with the Dart.

segments.title: Why rugged reliability mattered more than a sleek airframe
segments.summary: A comparison with a smoother Blackburn design explains why the proven Dart's rugged reliability remained valuable in single-engine naval aviation.
segments.body: The video contrasts the Velos and Dart family with a visibly smoother, more aerodynamic Blackburn aircraft, raising the question of why the older-looking design persisted. Cost was part of the answer, but the central argument is that the Dart had already proved exceptionally reliable. For single-engine naval aircraft, that dependable ruggedness could outweigh the attraction of a more refined shape.

evidence.note: The presenter contrasts aircraft forms, then says cost and the Dart's established reliability explain Blackburn's appeal, especially for single-engine naval aircraft.

segments.title: Greek production and flexible Velos crew arrangements
segments.summary: Blackburn's Greek production work supported training-capable Velos aircraft while retaining a single-seat torpedo and coastal-defence role.
segments.body: In 1925 Blackburn took over completion of the Greek National Aircraft Factory and received a five-year operating contract using local labour. Two aircraft built for training had matching cockpits and dual controls, while the operational machines carried coastal-defence equipment including a Lewis gun behind the pilot. The second cockpit could support bombing, reconnaissance, or instructional sorties, but the Velos flew as a single-seater when carrying a torpedo.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Blackburn's Greek factory contract, dual-control training machines, coastal-defence equipment, and single-seat torpedo operation.
